title: Second-Order Synaptic Memory using Inherent Plasticity of Moiré Superlattices
authors: Tanweer Ahmed, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Fèlix Casanova, Luis E. Hueso
abstract: Achieving synaptic functionality electronically in a single-element quantum material is a fundamental challenge, as conventional methods rely on the introduction of extrinsic charge-traps or polar components. Here, we demonstrate that twisted double bilayer graphene (tDBLG) moiré superlattices, composed purely of carbon, exhibit electronic hysteresis and plasticity in presence of twist-angle disorder. Inversion symmetry breaking at the moiré length scales also gives rise to second-order nonlinear electrical response via disorder-mediated extrinsic mechanisms. Such second-order nonlinearity is highly tunable in both sign and magnitude by varying carrier concentration and vertical displacement field. We harness the coexistence of electronic plasticity and second-order nonlinearity to realize a second-order synaptic memory device. Our findings establish strained moiré carbon systems as a powerful new platform for energy-efficient neuromorphic computing, demonstrating that complex electronic functionality can emerge purely from symmetry breaking physics in a single-element material.
summary: This work reports on using moiré superlattices in tDBLG to create a novel, energy-efficient synaptic memory. By combining inherent electronic hysteresis (plasticity) with a tunable second-order nonlinear electrical response, the authors show that complex neuromorphic functions can emerge purely from symmetry breaking physics in a single-element carbon material.
main_problem: The core challenge is achieving electronic synaptic functionality in a single-element quantum material without relying on external charge traps or polar components.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated a second-order synaptic memory device by harnessing the combination of electronic plasticity (hysteresis) and tunable second-order nonlinear electrical response in moiré superlattices.
method: Measurements involved using synchronized lock-in amplifiers to probe both first-order ($\omega$) and second-order ($2\omega$) voltage drops while varying carrier density and vertical displacement field.
model_or_system: The system is twisted double bilayer graphene (tDBLG) moiré superlattices, which are purely carbon-based and exhibit electronic properties governed by moiré periodicity and strain-induced symmetry breaking.
key_observables: Electronic hysteresis in resistance ($R_S$), second-order nonlinear electrical response ($\propto V_{2\omega}/I_{ds}^{2\omega}$), and multi-level synaptic states.
important_parameters: Twist angle disorder, carrier concentration ($n$), vertical displacement field ($D$), and temperature ($T$).
main_assumptions: The observed hysteresis is attributed to inhomogeneous flexoelectric coupling arising from intrinsic strain gradients, rather than simple ferroelectricity or extrinsic traps.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate $R_S$ vs. $n$ showing superlattice band filling anomalies, $R_S$ vs. $D$ showing pronounced hysteresis loops, and operational diagrams demonstrating synaptic potentiation/depression cycles using $V_{2\omega}$ measurements.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the fundamental electronic plasticity via $R_S$ hysteresis, to confirming the intrinsic second-order nonlinearity, and finally demonstrating the combination of these two effects to realize a functional, low-power, second-order synaptic memory device.
why_interesting: The emergence of complex, tunable electronic functionality (second-order nonlinearity and plasticity) purely from symmetry breaking physics in a single-element 2D material is highly relevant to understanding emergent quantum phenomena in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: 𝑫 dependent hysteresis in strained tDBLG superlattice: a, the cross-section of the stacking sequence of tDBLG devices is schematically shown, the twist angle (𝜃) is indicated. b, the measurement geometry is schematically illustrated. Longitudinal (𝑉𝑥𝑥) and transverse (𝑉𝑥𝑦) voltages in both the first (𝜔) and second (2𝜔) harmonics are measured simultaneously under applied current bias (𝐼𝜔). c, sheet resistance (𝑅𝑆) vs. carrier density (𝑛) characteristics at vertical fixed displacement field (𝐷) ≈0 from three tDBLG samples are presented. 𝑅𝑠 peak at integer superlattice filling (𝑛/𝑛𝑠) appears to be a combination of at least two 𝑅𝑆 maxima (labeled with arrows). This indicates...

Figure 2: 𝑫-dependent hysteresis of the band structure: a, the 𝑅𝑆,𝐶𝑁𝑃−𝐷 hysteresis loops collected from sample s0.7 at different 𝑇, which are vertically shifted. The baselines are shown as dashed lines. red (black) traces present the ascending (descending) 𝐷 sweep directions (indicated with arrows). b, estimated band-gap (𝐸𝑔) vs. 𝐷 data for both sweep direction is presented. The hysteresis in the 𝐸𝑔−𝐷/𝜀0 characteristics indicates the appearance of an internal vertical polarization (𝑃𝑖𝑛). The red (orange) and black (blue) circles represent the same value of 𝐸𝑔, for ascending and descending 𝐷 sweeps, respectively, However, they exhibit significantly different values of 𝑅𝑆,𝐶𝑁𝑃. c, ∆𝐸𝑔 between...

Figure 3: Second-order nonlinear electrical response from tDBLG: a, The black and red traces are 𝑅𝑆−𝑛 data from sample s0.7, measured in two measurement configurations with opposite polarities of current and voltage probes (black and red schematics in the insets). b, The top and bottom panels show the 𝑉𝑥𝑦 2𝜔/𝐼𝑑𝑠

Figure 4: Second-order plasticity of tDBLG: a and b, 𝑛 vs. 𝐷/𝜀0 phase spaces of 𝑉𝑥𝑥𝜔 and 𝑉𝑥𝑥2𝜔, respectively, close to 𝑛/𝑛𝑠= 1 is shown from sample s0.7. Sweep directions are marked with green arrows. The synaptic memory action was executed along the vertical dashed line. c, The top and the bottom panels present the sweep range dependent hysteresis 𝑉𝑥𝑥2𝜔 and 𝑉𝑥𝑦 2𝜔, respectively, for a few different 𝐷 sweep range. The data of different sweep ranges are vertically shifted. The sweep directions are indicated with arrows. The light and dark traces indicate data for symmetric and asymmetric sweep ranges. The low and the high states (at 𝐷/𝜀0 = 0.1 Vnm-1) are marked. Also see SI section 15 and 17...

Figure 5: Second-order synaptic memory operation: a and b, The depression and potentiation cycles of the second order synaptic response is presented, respectively. The timeseries of the sequence of negative (constant) and positive (of varying magnitude) triangular pulsed pulses are shown in the bottom panels. The top and the middle panels present the synaptic response of 𝑉𝑥𝑦 2𝜔 and 𝑉𝑥𝑥2𝜔, respectively. c, the long-term synaptic plasticity of the second-order response (𝑉𝑥𝑥2𝜔 and 𝑉𝑥𝑦 2𝜔) over several potentiation (red arrows) and depression cycles are presented (black arrows). Also see SI section 17 for synaptic operation in sample s1.4.
